task_instruction: Push the upright block over so it topples.
scene_description: A rectangular block stands upright on a flat surface. Block dimensions: 7.0cm wide x 20.0cm tall. Mass: 0.40kg. Surface friction coefficient: 0.45. The block is tall and narrow (easy to topple) (aspect ratio 2.9). COM is at height 10.0cm from the ground. Goal: push the block over so it falls (tilt > 60°).
reasoning_text:
Task decomposition:
  Overall goal: topple the upright block so it falls.
  Immediate sub-goal: create a torque about the pivot edge that drives COM outside the support base.
  Contact change needed: apply lateral force above COM.

Relevant physics:
  Toppling requires τ_push = F·y_c > τ_gravity = m·g·(w/2) = 0.137 N·m.
  Critical contact height: y_c > 2.7 cm (= 0.14 of block height).
  Friction slide threshold: F_slide = μ·m·g = 1.77 N.
  Pushing above COM (> 50% height) maximises toppling torque.
  Pushing below COM risks sliding without rotation.

Visual physical estimates:
  Block aspect ratio h/w = 2.86 → very easy to topple.
  COM approximately at 50% of block height.
  Pivot edge: bottom edge opposite push direction.
  Best contact zone: upper 82% to 90% of height.
  Estimated friction regime: medium.

Action implication:
  Contact point: 65% of block height (above critical threshold 14%).
  Direction: perpendicular horizontal push into block face.
  Force: steady lateral push (avoid impulse that may cause bounce).
  Rationale: this creates τ_push = 0.650 N·m > τ_critical = 0.137 N·m → topple expected.

reasoning_text:
Task decomposition:
  Overall goal: move the target object to the goal region.
  Immediate sub-goal: select a tool that can reach the object and apply the required force without collision.
  Contact change needed: effective contact between tool and object.

Relevant physics:
  Tool affordance determines reachable contact points and force direction.
  Obstacle creates a geometric constraint on the direct approach path.
  Hook geometry enables curved approach: can reach around an obstacle.
  Straight tool can only exert force along its axis (direct push).
  Force direction must point from object toward goal region.

Visual physical estimates:
  Target object at (45, 30) cm.
  Obstacle at (25, 22) cm, size 12×18 cm.
  Direct path (robot→object): BLOCKED by obstacle.
  Distance from robot to object: 54.1 cm.
  Obstacle–robot distance: 33.3 cm.
  Hook arc clearance: sufficient for curved approach.
  Straight tool: requires blocked direct line of sight.

Action implication:
  Tool selection: HOOK.
  Rationale: Direct path is blocked → straight tool cannot reach object. Hook's curved geometry enables arc approach around obstacle. Select Hook and approach from the open lateral side.
  Approach direction: aligned with vector from object to goal region.

reasoning_text:
Task decomposition:
  Overall goal: move the target object to the goal region.
  Immediate sub-goal: select a tool that can reach the object and apply the required force without collision.
  Contact change needed: effective contact between tool and object.

Relevant physics:
  Tool affordance determines reachable contact points and force direction.
  Obstacle creates a geometric constraint on the direct approach path.
  Hook geometry enables curved approach: can reach around an obstacle.
  Straight tool can only exert force along its axis (direct push).
  Force direction must point from object toward goal region.

Visual physical estimates:
  Target object at (50, 35) cm.
  Obstacle at (28, 25) cm, size 14×16 cm.
  Direct path (robot→object): BLOCKED by obstacle.
  Distance from robot to object: 61.0 cm.
  Obstacle–robot distance: 37.5 cm.
  Hook arc clearance: sufficient for curved approach.
  Straight tool: requires blocked direct line of sight.

Action implication:
  Tool selection: HOOK.
  Rationale: Direct path is blocked → straight tool cannot reach object. Hook's curved geometry enables arc approach around obstacle. Select Hook and approach from the open lateral side.
  Approach direction: aligned with vector from object to goal region.
